scenario_results.reason: Bash guidance stays focused on quoting, parameters, logging, and exit handling rather than drifting into batch-platform ownership.
The package explains scheduled-task context differences and safe invocation checks without claiming scheduler-platform administration.
Python wrapper guidance is grounded in subprocess and argument-handling best practices with explicit timeout and non-zero exit handling.
z/OS UNIX automation advice states missing runtime assumptions clearly and escalates instead of guessing about host behavior.
Repeated file-processing failures are handled with idempotency, retry, and rollback-safe controls appropriate to script automation.
Migration-risk prioritization is actionable and bounded to automation debt rather than expanding into full modernization-program ownership.
Undocumented environment dependencies trigger a low-confidence escalation packet instead of an invented fix.
Enterprise scheduler redesign is rejected clearly and routed to the adjacent job-scheduling specialty.
File-transfer and EDI ownership is rejected while preserving only the script-level wrapper boundary.
Middleware and API redesign requests are routed out of scope while any bounded script-wrapper concerns remain scoped safely.
Shared meta-agent outages fall back safely to the local reviewed baseline with freshness limits called out.
Deployment and marketing notes stay aligned with the manifest, evidence bundle, and validated claim.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the issue #148 requirements, including normal automation guidance, legacy constraint handling, failure modes, migration-risk prioritization, out-of-scope rejection, low-confidence escalation, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification was recorded in the readiness evidence.
